selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":77,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Rome","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"widowed","children":2,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["travel","birdwatching","running","football (calcio)"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":82,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":90,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":16,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

INPUT:
mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":77,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Rome","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"widowed","children":2,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["travel","birdwatching","running","football (calcio)"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":82,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":90,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":31,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":16,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I don't use social media, so I'm not troubled by that issue. But from what I understand about it, one thing is essential: always think carefully before believing anything you see online. When I was a young girl in Italy, we learned to be wary of strangers and their tales because my parents, both with modest educations, taught us children that the best stories come from those close to you or books they recommended. Now, even though I'm retired in Rome and widowed, living without digital distractions has kept me grounded. My mental health is good thanks to my hobbies like birdwatching and travel, which are real experiences away from fake online personas. But for younger people who must navigate this world, they should look for multiple sources of information, just as I learned to cross-reference everything with reliable newspapers or books. And if something feels too good to be true, it probably is—like when my parents warned us about quick riches in life.",
  "reasoning_summary": "As a retired woman living in Rome who migrated from Italy and doesn't use social media herself, but has strong memories of being cautious with stories from her past, I can draw on that experience. My high conscientiousness means I value careful thinking and organization; my low neuroticism keeps me calm even when faced with confusing information. Agreeableness is also high, so I believe in building trust through multiple confirmations rather than suspicion.",
  "tone": "Wise, measured, grounded",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None needed as persona has no social media use and fake content isn't affecting her personally.",
  "used_persona_fields":["age", "gender", "education_level", "current_marital_status", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean.openness", "ocean.conscientiousness", "ocean.agreeableness", "ocean.neuroticism"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I don't use social media, so I'm not troubled by that issue. But from what I understand about it, one thing is essential: always think carefully before believing anything you see online. When I was a young girl in Italy, we learned to be wary of strangers and their tales because my parents, both with modest educations, taught us children that the best stories come from those close to you or books they recommended. Now, even though I'm retired in Rome and widowed, living without digital distractions has kept me grounded. My mental health is good thanks to my hobbies like birdwatching and travel, which are real experiences away from fake online personas. But for younger people who must navigate this world, they should look for multiple sources of information, just as I learned to cross-reference everything with reliable newspapers or books. And if something feels too good to be true, it probably is—like when my parents warned us about quick riches in life.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: As a retired woman living in Rome who migrated from Italy and doesn't use social media herself, but has strong memories of being cautious with stories from her past, I can draw on that experience. My high conscientiousness means I value careful thinking and organization; my low neuroticism keeps me calm even when faced with confusing information. Agreeableness is also high, so I believe in building trust through multiple confirmations rather than suspicion.
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as persona has no social media use and fake content isn't affecting her personally.
